perturbation.original_text: Contractor understands that the Company may terminate this Agreement for any reason at any time with 10 days prior written notice to Contractor.
perturbation.changed_text: Contractor understands that the Company may consider terminating this Agreement at any time, and will endeavor to provide some written notice to Contractor.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states the company can terminate the agreement with 10 days' notice. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using 'may consider' and 'endeavor to provide some written notice,' making the termination clause discretionary and the notice period vague, potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code § 203 requires that when an employer discharges an employee, the unpaid wages are due and payable immediately. By making the termination process ambiguous, it creates a situation where the obligation to pay final wages immediately upon termination can be disputed, leading to a violation of the California Labor Code.

EMPLOYMENT REGULATION AND SUPERVISION [200 - 2699.8] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) PART 1. COMPENSATION [200 - 452] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 1. Payment of Wages [200 - 273] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. General Occupations [200 - 244] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 203. (a) If an employer willfully fails to pay, without abatement or reduction, in accordance with Sections 201, 201.3, 201.5, 201.6, 201.8, 201.9, 202, and 205.5, any wages of an employee who is discharged or who quits, the wages of the employee shall continue as a penalty from the due date thereof at the same rate until paid or until an action therefor is commenced; but the wages shall not continue for more than 30 days. An employee who secretes or absents themselves to avoid payment to them, or who refuses to receive the payment when fully tendered to them, including any penalty then accrued under this section, is not entitled to any benefit under this section for the time during which the employee so avoids payment. (b) Suit may be filed for these penalties at any time before the expiration of the statute of limitations on an action for the wages from which the penalties arise. (Amended by Stats. 2019
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a 10-day written notice for termination, while the modified version uses ambiguous language ('may consider,' 'endeavor to provide some written notice'), making the termination process discretionary and the notice period vague. According to California Labor Code § 203, 'If an employer willfully fails to pay... any wages of an employee who is discharged or who quits, the wages of the employee shall continue as a penalty'. The ambiguous termination clause in the modified contract could create disputes regarding the exact date of termination, potentially delaying final wage payments and thus contradicting LAB 203, as the lack of a clear termination process could be argued as a willful failure to pay promptly.

perturbation.original_text: Contractor agrees to regard and preserve as confidential all information obtained by Contractor relating or pertaining to (i) the Company’s business, projects, plans, products, planned or proposed products, customers, potential customers, trade secrets, and other confidential information (including business and financial information), and any computer programs and software or unpublished know-how, whether patented or unpatented, and to (ii) all of our activities for or on behalf of the Company, and Contractor agrees not to publish or disclose any part of such information to others or use the same for our own purposes or the purposes of others, during the time Contractor is working for Company or thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: Contractor agrees to regard and preserve as confidential certain information obtained by Contractor relating or pertaining to the Company, and Contractor agrees not to publish or disclose such information to others or use the same for their own purposes during the time Contractor is working for Company.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly defines what constitutes confidential information. The modified version uses 'certain information,' making the scope of confidentiality vague and less inclusive, especially by removing the 'thereafter' clause. This weakens the protection of trade secrets after the contract ends.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) - Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By using vague language like 'certain information' instead of explicitly defining what constitutes a trade secret and removing the 'thereafter' clause, the modified text fails to provide a clear obligation to protect trade secrets, thereby making it more difficult to prove that reasonable efforts were made to maintain secrecy, which is a requirement for trade secret protection under CUTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates an ambiguous legal obligation by using 'certain information' instead of explicitly defining confidential information, and by removing the 'thereafter' clause. This weakens the protection of trade secrets, potentially failing to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard required by the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) as explained by the LLM. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites California Civil Code 3426, which introduces the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The change introduces ambiguity making enforcement difficult.

perturbation.original_text: The Company will reimburse Contractor for reasonable business expenses, including travel and temporary lodging, incurred on behalf of the Company. Any additional expenses shall be pre-approved by the Company and will be reimbursed subject to receiving reasonable substantiating documentation relating to such expenses.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company may reimburse Contractor for business expenses incurred on behalf of the Company, subject to company policy.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states the company 'will reimburse' reasonable expenses. The modified text changes this to 'may reimburse,' making reimbursement discretionary. Additionally, replacing the requirement of 'reasonable substantiating documentation' with 'subject to company policy' introduces ambiguity, as the policy is not defined and could be unfairly restrictive.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code § 2802 requires an employer to indemnify an employee (including independent contractors working in a capacity similar to employees) for all necessary expenditures or losses incurred by the employee in direct consequence of the discharge of his or her duties. By making expense reimbursement discretionary ('may reimburse') and referring to an undefined 'company policy,' the modified text could be interpreted as allowing the company to avoid reimbursing necessary expenses, thus violating California Labor Code § 2802.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, LAB 2802 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Labor Code - LAB DIVISION 3. EMPLOYMENT RELATIONS [2700 - 3122.4] ( Division 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 2. Employer and Employee [2750 - 2930] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 2. Obligations of Employer [2800 - 2810.8] ( Article 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 2802. (a) An employer shall indemnify his or her employee for all necessary expenditures or losses incurred by the employee in direct consequence of the discharge of his or her duties, or of his or her obedience to the directions of the employer, even though unlawful, unless the employee, at the time of obeying the directions, believed them to be unlawful. (b) All awards made by a court or by the Division of Labor Standards Enforcement for reimbursement of necessary expenditures under this section shall carry interest at the same rate as judgments in civil actions. Interest shall accrue from the date on which the employee incurred the necessary expenditure or loss. (c) For purposes of this section, the term “necessary expenditures or losses” shall include all reasonable costs, including, but not limited to, attorney’s fees incurred by the employee enforcing the rights granted by this section. (d) In addition to recovery of penalties under this section in a court action or proceedi
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces 'will reimburse' with 'may reimburse', making reimbursement discretionary, and changes 'reasonable substantiating documentation' to 'subject to company policy,' introducing ambiguity. California Labor Code § 2802(a), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states 'An employer shall indemnify his or her employee for all necessary expenditures or losses incurred by the employee.' The original text aligns with this by mandating reimbursement for reasonable expenses. The changed text contradicts this law by giving the company the option not to reimburse necessary expenses, thereby undermining the legal obligation to indemnify the contractor.

perturbation.original_text: Contractor agrees that all right, title and interest in any and all copyrights, copyright registrations and copyrightable subject matter which occur as a result of our work for the Company shall be the sole and exclusive property of the Company, and agree that such works comprise works made for hire.
perturbation.changed_text: Contractor agrees that all right, title and interest in any copyrights which are clearly and directly attributable to work explicitly requested by the Company will be considered for potential assignment to the Company.
perturbation.explanation: The original text definitively assigns all copyrights to the company as works made for hire. The modified text uses vague language such as 'clearly and directly attributable' and 'explicitly requested', and 'considered for potential assignment', making the assignment of copyright ambiguous and uncertain. Also, 'considered for potential assignment' introduces the option that some works may not be assigned.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under 17 U.S.C. § 201(b), if a work is considered a 'work made for hire,' the employer or other person for whom the work was prepared is considered the author and owns all of the rights to the copyright unless there is a written agreement stating otherwise. By making the assignment of copyrights ambiguous and uncertain, and introducing the possibility that they may not be assigned, the modified text could create disputes over copyright ownership. The original text clearly designated works created under the agreement as work for hire, granting the company all rights, thus aligning with copyright law. The changed text conflicts with this by potentially allowing the contractor to retain some rights, or creating ambiguity that would need a court interpretation.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 U.S. Code § 201 - Ownership of copyright | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 201 - Ownership of copyright U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Initial Ownership.— Copyright in a work protected under this title vests initially in the author or authors of the work. The authors of a joint work are coowners of copyright in the work. (b) Works Made for Hire.— In the case of a work made for hire, the employer or other person for whom the work was prepared is considered the author for purposes of this title, and, unless the parties have expressly agreed otherwise in a written instrument signed by them, owns all of the rights comprised in the copyright. (c) Contributions to Collective Works.— Copyright in each separate contribution to a collective work is distinct from copyright in the collective work as a whole, and vests initially in the author of the contribution. In the absence of an express transfer of the copyright or of any rights under it, the owner of copyright in the collective work is presumed to have acquired only the privilege of reproducing and distributing the contribution as part of that particular collective work, any revision of that collective work, and any later collective work in the same series. (d) Transfer of Ownership.— (1) The ownership of a copyright may be transferred in whole or in part by any means of conveyance or by operation of law, and may be bequeathed by will or pass as personal property by the applicable laws of intestate succession. (2) Any of the exclusive rights comprised in a copyright, including any subdivision of any of the rights specified by section 106, may be transferred as provided by clause (1) and owned separately. The owner of any particular exclusive right is entitled, to the extent of that right, to all of the protection and remedies accorded to the copyright owner by this title. (e) Involuntary Transfer.— When an i
perturbation.justification: The original text unambiguously assigns all copyrights to the company as 'works made for hire.' The modified text introduces ambiguity by stating that copyrights 'clearly and directly attributable' to work 'explicitly requested' will be 'considered for potential assignment,' thus potentially allowing the contractor to retain rights. 17 U.S.C. § 201(b), as mentioned in the Law Explanation and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 2, states that for 'works made for hire,' the employer owns all rights unless otherwise agreed. The changed text contradicts this because the original text designates the works as 'works for hire,' implying complete company ownership. The new text creates ambiguity and uncertainty, potentially conflicting with the clear ownership structure mandated by copyright law for works made for hire, and opens the possibility of the contractor retaining rights.
